Hey JB here, time for your daily motivation slash skills training. Gotta talk to you about something. Hall of the University study. I assume you've been through that lesson right now. Tell me that's not the weirdest thing, right? So I say this in 1988, it's October 1988, right? And I say you got four seconds, right? Well, they correct me 30 years late and they say it's seven seconds. That's not the part that freaks me out. What freaks me out is the other part of the equation. They say it takes you seven subsequent impressions to change someone's mind about you if you make a negative first impression. You know, I guess because they're just not business people at Harvard or not salespeople, you don't get seven shots. That was what was so interesting about that, like, okay, I get that. What's the context of that? You don't get more than one, two tops to redeem yourself. You have one chance to make a first impression and once it's made, it's indelible. It just hits people so viscerally that it's really almost, I would never say impossible, but it's very, very difficult to recolor from. So what do you do? Well, it starts by you just remembering that there are no free moments in the conversation. In the first 10, 15 seconds, you have to be on. You've got to be on. And the danger is that this habituation that occurs because, you know, here's what happens. I never mean, and I want to be clear about this. I never am saying that you should be unethically over-enthusiastic and over-excited. That's not what I mean at all. I wouldn't want you to be disingenuous. That sounds, A, it's not right and B, it won't work. What I'm talking about is this almost overarching pressure on all salespeople is that I'm speaking to new people every day. Sometimes I can talk to 100 people a day, first-time conversations. And for me to keep saying things with the same level of enthusiasm and excitement as the first person I ever mentioned this to, that's a skill that's not so easy to master. It's a continue to sound enthusiastic. It's almost like the 137th picture the day you're like, I feel stupid, you know, hey Bill, I hear, and you're like, oh my God, I sound like this. It's Bill's first time hearing it. You get that? Every person that you speak to, it's the first time they've heard your voice, they're receiving the information. What a great salesperson does and what the straight line insists on. That's why I point that, I spent almost a whole lesson on this, is this idea that habituation occurs, that it's not that you are not enthusiastic about your product or that I want you to act over enthusiastic, because remember it's about bottles of enthusiasm. What happens is that over time you lose the spark. You tend to lose the spark that you initially felt when you first found out about all the features and benefits, how awesome your product is. Everyone like, listen, if you're not working in a solid company, it shouldn't be there. So you mostly, guys that work in solid companies selling good products, right? Well, I can't say, I don't know what you're exactly selling, but when I receive an email from somebody and get them all day, 10 a day, you change my life. The straight line system is unbelievable. I closed X number of sales, I made this, I get them all day long and you know I do, because you're experiencing it for yourself, right? When I read those, it like makes my heart skip a beat. Like, it pumps me up. And in that moment, I'm like, if anyone I spoke to about my product, I'd be even more excited. I'd say, oh my God, this is the best, I have such self-belief in my product, right? Well, one of the things I do really well is that I can bring that level of enthusiasm to the table in every single presentation. No matter how I feel inside, no matter how many times I've said it in one day, in that moment I can snap and get myself, and I want to state, that's the next module, but I'm told here is just being aware that you have to bring that energy, that enthusiasm to every single sale. Don't think that because you've said it a hundred times or a thousand times, there's anything weird or strange or wrong about you regaining that original enthusiasm you felt with every single presentation. That's what I want for you. Now I go through individual tonalities in the advanced tone section, I'll show you how to really muster each individual one, but it starts with the mindset. It starts with just realizing and being okay with the fact that as a salesperson, as an influencer, I have to recreate that same level of enthusiasm, each pitch. I got one shot to capture their attention. If I do it incorrectly, I am fighting this massive uphill battle. If I do it right, if I do it well, make the correct first impression, it's a downhill slide. I got gravity inertia on my side versus working heavily against me. Remember that and just actively fight against habituation. Just say to yourself, the first step is always just being aware that this is like, I got to come out, I got to come out of the gate strong, bottom line. Make that your mantra today. I'm coming out of the gate strong with every presentation. Those first four seconds, they matter for everything. Straight lines end to end, right? Remember those first four seconds, they either set you up for success or set you up for dismal failure, all right? Love you all. Talk to you tomorrow.
